material_value.title: Material Value
description: An estimate of the relative worth of a piece or pawn, which can be used as a heuristic for the purposes of
evaluating exchanges and calculating the overall strength of a position.
The standard values are as follows:

value: Piece
Value
Pawn
Knight
Bishop
Rook
Queen

While the above numbers are sufficient in most cases, the value of an individual piece or pawn can vary depending

development.title: Development
description: The process of moving (non-pawn) pieces from their starting squares to squares where they are more
and the king to a square where it is
It is one of the biggest priorities of
and can remain a priority throughout the course of a game if pieces are left undeveloped for long enough.
In most openings, the following hierarchy is broadly observed for development:

value: pieces are developed first. As these pieces are of lower value than their heavy counterparts,
they are harder for the opponent to harass since only pawns can threaten to win material in a
1-for-1 exchange with them.
This makes them ideal as part of the vanguard along with
and
fighting for
in the
- another major priority of the opening phase.
Additionally, it is necessary due to simple logistics to develop at least one knight and one
bishop before castling can be carried out.
The king is castled out of the centre of the board and towards one of the corners, where it
is far easier to maintain a stronghold as threats can come from fewer possible directions, and
where conflict over central control cannot put the king at risk by proximity.
If opting to castle queenside, the queen must necessarily also be developed beforehand.
Castling can be carried out immediately after clearing enough pieces out of the way, can
be purposefully deferred in favour of achieving other strategic goals, or can even be eschewed
entirely; however, it is necessary in the latter 2 cases (and particularly in the last case) to take
into account how the opponent might attempt to
a king left in the centre too long, or this can lead to a swift loss.
is an additional possibility, and is often carried out as a fallback if standard castling has been
rendered impossible by the events of the game.

A bishop placed in the centre can see to all four corners, and a knight will not have its movement
blocked by the edges of the board.
This makes the centre a
stronghold, and fighting for
